metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly require that new `overlap_*` fields added to `VerifyConfig` are also registered in `_SECTIONS['verify']` with an appropriate coercer (e.g. `_as_float` for `overlap_conf_threshold`, `_as_int` for `overlap_surface_cap`).
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` fields on `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, but every `VerifyConfig` field requires a parallel coercer entry in `_SECTIONS['verify']` (the coercion dict at line 422–438 of `_config_schema.py`). The plan does not mention this required parallel update. Without it, the config parser will warn-and-drop these keys at load time (or hard-error under `strict=True`).
findings.location: Scope section: '`overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` fields on `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `[tool.rebar][verify]`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Also update `_SECTIONS["verify"]` in `_config_schema.py` with coercers: `"overlap_conf_threshold": lambda v, k: _as_float(v, k, minimum=0.0, maximum=1.0)` and `"overlap_surface_cap": lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=1)`.' Add an AC that verifies setting these keys in a rebar.toml round-trips through `Config.from_mapping`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` from `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` to `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (the correct home for LLM-operation tunables), and update the Scope accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan places `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` fields on `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`. However, `_config_schema.py`'s own module docstring explicitly states: '`llm.*` keys live in the optional `rebar.llm` layer (not here) so the stdlib core never depends on the agents extra.' These are LLM-operation tunables, not core verify-gate booleans. Placing them on `VerifyConfig` contradicts the module's design constraint and names the wrong edit target; `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` is the appropriate home.
findings.location: Scope section: '`overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` fields on `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `[tool.rebar][verify]`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate `_SECTIONS['verify']` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as a required edit target, with coercer entries for `overlap_conf_threshold` (float, 0–1) and `overlap_surface_cap` (int, ≥1).
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as an edit target for adding fields to `VerifyConfig`, but does not enumerate the two companion tables `_SECTIONS['verify']` (the coercion dict) and `_SECTION_CLASSES` (the class map) that must also be updated in the same file. Without entries in `_SECTIONS['verify']`, any user-supplied `overlap_conf_threshold` or `overlap_surface_cap` value will be silently dropped by `coerce_sparse` (with a warning), not validated or applied.
findings.location: Scope section: '`overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` fields on `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `_SECTIONS['verify']`: `'overlap_conf_threshold': lambda v, k: _as_float(v, k, minimum=0.0, maximum=1.0)` and `'overlap_surface_cap': lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=1)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the eval AC's precision/recall floor language with the existing scorer infrastructure: either (a) implement `precision_floor` and `recall_floor` aggregate scorers in `eval_scorers.py` + register them, or (b) restate the ACs using the existing per-case `recall_on_*` / `no_fire_on_*` scorer vocabulary that the harness can already execute.
findings.finding: The eval AC specifies 'precision ≥0.8 AND recall ≥0.6' as numeric aggregate floors, but the existing eval harness (eval_scorers.REGISTRY) has no precision or recall aggregate scorer — only per-case _recall (should-fire cases must fire) and _no_fire (should-not-fire cases must not fire). Implementing aggregate precision/recall over the labeled fixture requires new scorer infrastructure (counting TP/FP/FN across all cases in the dataset, not per-case) that the plan does not mention. The plan's eval design is therefore non-executable with existing scorer vocabulary without adding new scorer implementations.
findings.location: Eval section: 'Eval fixture (≥12 labeled pairs) gives precision ≥0.8 AND recall ≥0.6'; Testing section: 'BOTH a precision floor (≥0.8) AND a recall floor (≥0.6) on the fixture'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` out of `VerifyConfig` / `[tool.rebar][verify]` and into the LLM-layer config (`LLMConfig` / `[tool.rebar.llm]`), consistent with how the codebase designates `llm` as a reserved section owned by the optional agents layer.
findings.finding: overlap_conf_threshold and overlap_surface_cap are proposed as overlap_* fields on VerifyConfig in core _config_schema.py, but VerifyConfig is the completion/plan-review/claim gate config and the codebase already designates the 'llm' key as a _RESERVED_SECTION for the optional LLM layer. Placing retrieval-stage tunables inside the core typed config couples a new LLM-layer feature to the stdlib core that is explicitly kept free of agent-layer dependencies.
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap_conf_threshold, overlap_surface_cap as overlap_* fields on VerifyConfig (src/rebar/_config_schema.py, [tool.rebar][verify])'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_conf_threshold: float = 0.7` and `overlap_surface_cap: int = 3` as fields on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, resolved via `_llm_float`/`_llm_int` in `from_env`, consistent with existing `max_tokens`/`timeout_s` tunables.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the relation count: change Scope's 'the 4-way classification' to 'the 5-way classification' (or explicitly justify why one relation is collapsed/omitted in the prompt).
findings.finding: The Scope section instructs the rubric prompt to 'encode the 4-way classification' but the What section defines a 5-value enum (`duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated`). An implementer writing the rubric prompt has contradictory guidance about how many classification buckets to include.
findings.location: What section (relation enum) vs Scope section (rubric prompt body requirements)
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the 4-way classification' in the Scope rubric-prompt body requirements to 'the 5-way classification (duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the prompt-authoring instruction: change 'the 4-way classification' in Scope to 'the 5-way classification' (or explicitly name all five values) so it matches the `relation` enum in the `overlap_verdict` schema.
findings.finding: The Scope section instructs that the prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification', but the What section defines a 5-value CLOSED enum {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}. An executor authoring `overlap_judge.md` receives contradictory cardinality for the classification they must teach the model.
findings.location: Scope section ('whose body MUST encode: the 4-way classification') vs. What section ('relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'the 4-way classification' with 'the 5-way classification (duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | related_distinct | unrelated)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or replace the optional single-ordering cascade pre-pass: it reintroduces position bias at the pre-filter stage and can suppress genuine duplicates before the two-ordering confirm runs; if call-count reduction is needed, consider a symmetric cheap filter (e.g., embedding similarity threshold applied before ANY LLM call, which is ordering-independent) rather than a single-ordering LLM pre-pass.
findings.finding: The optional single-ordering cascade pre-pass reintroduces the exact position-bias failure mode the dual-ordering main pass was designed to eliminate: if A→B judges a pair 'unrelated' but B→A would judge it 'duplicates', the pre-pass silently drops a genuine duplicate before the confirmatory two-ordering confirm ever runs — undermining the core safety invariant ('position-bias disagreement fails safe toward distinct' presupposes BOTH orderings are observed, not that one ordering gates whether the second is attempted).
findings.location: What section — 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious `unrelated` before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single-ordering LLM pre-pass with an ordering-independent pre-filter (e.g., a minimum cosine similarity threshold on the S5 digests) that drops pairs without invoking LLM ordering at all; any pair that clears the filter then proceeds to the full 2-ordering confirm. This preserves the position-bias safety invariant while still cutting calls.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the fixture format in the plan (e.g., one YAML/JSONL file per pair with fields `query_digest`, `candidate_digest`, `ground_truth_relation`, `notes`) so executors and reviewers can verify the ≥12-pair eval AC concretely.
findings.finding: The fixture format for `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` is described as 'format documented' but the plan does not specify what the format is (e.g., field names, file type, labeling schema). An executor must invent the format.
findings.location: Testing section: 'a LABELED fixture (≥12 pairs at tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/, format documented)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Fixture format' subsection under Testing, e.g.: each file in `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` is a YAML with keys `{query_digest: str, candidate_digest: str, ground_truth_relation: 'duplicates'|'supersedes'|'depends_on'|'related_distinct'|'unrelated', shared_artifact: str|null, notes: str}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the numeric range for `confidence` (e.g., `float in [0.0, 1.0]`) to the `overlap_verdict` schema definition and add a default value for `overlap_conf_threshold` (e.g., `0.7`) in the Scope/Config section.
findings.finding: The `overlap_verdict` schema defines `confidence: number` but never specifies the numeric range (e.g., 0.0–1.0), and `overlap_conf_threshold` (the config key) has no stated default value — unlike SURFACE_CAP (default 3) and K (default 20) which both have explicit defaults. An executor cannot write the Pydantic field validator, the prompt's range instruction, or the `_config_schema.py` default without inventing a number.
findings.location: What section ('confidence: number') and Scope section ('overlap_conf_threshold … as overlap_* fields on VerifyConfig')
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section schema, write `confidence: float  # 0.0–1.0`. In the Scope config block, write `overlap_conf_threshold: float = 0.7`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete `category` value for `overlap_judge.md` in the Scope section (e.g., `category: overlap` or `category: plan-review-pass`, whichever is correct for this prompt's routing).
findings.finding: The Scope section requires a `category` field in the `overlap_judge.md` prompt front-matter but never states what value to assign. Every existing reviewer prompt in the codebase has an explicit category value (e.g., `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`, `code-review-pass`). An executor must guess which category applies — an instruction-locality gap.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct 'the 4-way classification' in the Scope section to '5-way classification' (or explicitly explain the intended grouping if `related_distinct` and `unrelated` are intentionally merged in the prompt despite being separate enum values).
findings.finding: The Scope section states the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification', but the `relation` enum defined earlier in the plan has 5 distinct values (`duplicates`, `supersedes`, `depends_on`, `related_distinct`, `unrelated`). This internal inconsistency will cause implementers to write a prompt that classifies into 4 buckets rather than 5, likely collapsing `related_distinct` and `unrelated` into one terminal category and breaking the aggregation rule which distinguishes them.
findings.location: Scope section: 'the 4-way classification'; What section: 'relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the 4-way classification' to 'the 5-way classification (duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | related_distinct | unrelated)' and ensure the prompt template lists all five options explicitly.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: instrument LLM judge calls with at minimum a per-run abstain-rate counter and error-rate log (or metric), so operators can distinguish 'no overlaps found' from 'judge silently failed on all candidates'.
findings.finding: The plan specifies LLM errors are 'logged' but provides no requirement for metrics or traces on LLM call failures, latency, or the abstain rate — leaving operators unable to distinguish a healthy judge from one silently abstaining on every candidate due to a misconfiguration or degraded LLM endpoint.
findings.location: ## What — Error handling paragraph; ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In the Error handling spec, add: emit a structured log entry (or increment a metric) at the end of each judge run reporting `{total_candidates, abstained, surfaced, error_count}` so an elevated abstain_rate is detectable. Optionally add a warning log if abstain_rate > 0.5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit constraint to the `overlap_verdict` schema (or Pydantic validator) and to the `link_command` AC that the `relation` field in any surfaced finding MUST be a member of `{duplicates, supersedes, depends_on}` and that `related_distinct`/`unrelated` must never appear in a generated `link_command`.
findings.finding: The `overlap_verdict` `relation` enum includes two internal-only values (`related_distinct`, `unrelated`) that must never be passed to `rebar link`, but the schema definition and the AC for `link_command` formation do not explicitly exclude them. The plan's prose says 'the judge only ever suggests the three listed' but the schema itself does not encode this constraint, leaving it for the implementer to enforce outside the model.
findings.location: What section (overlap_verdict schema + 'only duplicates|supersedes|depends_on are surfaceable') and AC: 'link_command is a valid rebar link invocation'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section's schema registration note, add: 'Pydantic validator on the surfaced-finding output must assert relation in {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on} before constructing link_command.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a comment/docstring to the `overlap_verdict` schema definition (in Scope) that explicitly cross-references the aggregation rule, so the schema and the protocol it enforces are co-located for the implementing executor.
findings.finding: The dual-ordering aggregation rule (both agree + non-null shared_artifact + ≥CONF_THRESHOLD → surface; else downgrade to related_distinct) is stated only in the What prose section, but the `overlap_verdict` schema is defined in a separate schema block with no pointer to the rule. The executor implementing the Pydantic model and the judge.py aggregation logic must mentally reunite them. No AC test names explicitly check that the schema and the rule are co-implemented.
findings.location: What section (aggregation rule prose) vs. What section (schema definition block) and AC list
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section where overlap_verdict is registered, add a note: '# Both-orderings aggregation rule: surface only when A→B and B→A agree on the same surfaceable relation, both cite non-null shared_artifact, and both confidence >= CONF_THRESHOLD; else relation = related_distinct (not surfaced).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly resolve the optional cascade as IN or OUT of scope for this stage (e.g., 'OUT of scope for S6; tracked as future optimization' or add it to Scope with a corresponding AC).
findings.finding: The 'optional cascade' pre-pass is mentioned as a feature ('Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass…') but is not ranked as in-scope or explicitly deferred. No AC covers it, leaving its implementation status ambiguous for the executor.
findings.location: What section: 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious `unrelated` before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the Scope or Out section: 'The single-ordering pre-pass cascade is OUT of scope for this stage and may be added as a follow-on optimization.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add K (e.g. `overlap_candidate_cap`, default 20) to the explicit `overlap_*` VerifyConfig fields in the Scope section, or explicitly state that K is inherited from S5 and not re-configurable here.
findings.finding: AC5 states 'CONF_THRESHOLD/K/SURFACE_CAP are configurable' but the Scope section lists only `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` fields on VerifyConfig; K (candidate count) is never listed as a deliverable config field in Scope. This leaves K configurable per AC5 but unspecified as a schema deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section (VerifyConfig fields) vs AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_candidate_cap` (default 20) to the Scope VerifyConfig fields list, or add a note: 'K is owned by S5 config and not re-declared here; AC5's K configurability refers to the S5 field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default for `overlap_conf_threshold` in the plan (e.g. `default: 0.7`) so the executor does not have to invent one.
findings.finding: The `overlap_conf_threshold` config field has no stated default value, unlike SURFACE_CAP (default 3) and K (default 20). An executor implementing `_config_schema.py` must invent a default without guidance.
findings.location: Scope section: '`overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap` as `overlap_*` fields on `VerifyConfig`'; AC: '`CONF_THRESHOLD`/K/`SURFACE_CAP` are configurable'
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'default: 0.7' (or whichever value the research converged on) to the `overlap_conf_threshold` field description, mirroring how SURFACE_CAP and K state their defaults.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC/test for the optional cascade (e.g. 'cascade pre-pass does not drop pairs that would be surfaced by the 2-ordering confirm') or move the cascade description to a future-work note and remove it from the active What section.
findings.finding: The 'optional cascade' (cheap single-ordering pre-pass to drop obvious `unrelated` pairs before the 2-ordering confirm) is described as a feature in What but has no covering acceptance criterion and is absent from the Scope deliverables list, making it orphaned described work.
findings.location: What section ('Optional cascade' sentence) vs Testing section and AC1–AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test that constructs >SURFACE_CAP (e.g., 5) candidates all satisfying the aggregation rule with distinct confidence values, and asserts that the RETURNED findings are exactly the top-SURFACE_CAP by confidence (not merely that len ≤ SURFACE_CAP).
findings.finding: When more than SURFACE_CAP candidates pass the aggregation rule, the plan specifies 'highest-confidence results' are surfaced, but no test verifies that the correct top-N items are selected — only that the count is ≤SURFACE_CAP. A bug in the ranking/slicing logic (e.g., returning the lowest-confidence N, or an arbitrary N) would go undetected.
findings.location: Testing section — `tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py`; AC line '≤SURFACE_CAP (default 3) findings surface'
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_overlap_judge.py`, add `test_surface_cap_selects_highest_confidence`: feed 5 mock candidates with confidences [0.95, 0.90, 0.85, 0.80, 0.75] all passing both-orderings agreement; assert the returned 3 findings have confidences {0.95, 0.90, 0.85} and the 0.80/0.75 pairs are absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or defer the optional single-ordering pre-pass cascade from S6 scope until a measured call-volume problem justifies it; if retained, add an explicit AC and a unit test covering the pre-pass drop/promote logic.
findings.finding: The plan includes an 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious unrelated before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls' with zero scale evidence or profiling data cited. K=20 (default) → 2K=40 LLM calls/query is already stated as 'flat in corpus size.' The pre-pass adds a third mode of execution (single-ordering path) that requires separate testing and error-handling, for a saving the plan's own design already bounds at 40 calls — a premature-optimization pattern per the DSO decider set.
findings.location: What section: 'Optional cascade: a cheap single-ordering pre-pass drops obvious unrelated before the 2-ordering confirm, to cut calls'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` to the Scope's file list so the new package is importable.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/overlap/judge.py` as a deliverable inside a new package directory `src/rebar/llm/overlap/`, but omits `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py`. Python requires an `__init__.py` for the directory to be importable as `rebar.llm.overlap`; without it, `from rebar.llm.overlap.judge import ...` raises an `ImportError` (or silently creates a namespace package that may not behave as expected under the project's import conventions).
findings.location: Scope section: 'New `src/rebar/llm/overlap/judge.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` (may be empty) to the Scope's enumerated new files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` as an edit target in Scope and instruct the executor to regenerate it (via `python -m rebar.llm.prompting.prompts`) after adding `overlap_judge.md`.
findings.finding: Adding `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` to the reviewers directory will cause the existing `test_index_on_disk_matches_regenerated` test (in `tests/unit/test_prompt_index.py`) to fail in CI because the committed `index.json` will no longer match the regenerated one. This is an unenumerated consumer of the edit to the reviewers directory.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in Scope: 'After adding `overlap_judge.md`, regenerate `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` by running `python -m rebar.llm.prompting.prompts` and commit the updated file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the eval AC (e.g., `rebar eval run src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/overlap_judge.eval.yaml --assert precision>=0.8 recall>=0.6`) so the live LLM path is exercisable end-to-end and the precision/recall floors are machine-verifiable.
findings.finding: The eval AC ('precision ≥0.8 AND recall ≥0.6') has no concrete proving command. There is no specified CLI invocation, script, or harness that produces the precision/recall numbers an executor (or reviewer) can run to verify the AC is met.
findings.location: Testing section: 'Eval src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/overlap_judge.eval.yaml'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Eval fixture (≥12 labeled pairs) gives precision ≥0.8 AND recall ≥0.6'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the both-orderings aggregation rule, the abstain-as-safe-default pattern, and the rationale for the closed 5-way relation enum (why only 3 are surfaceable) so future maintainers understand the FP-control intent.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (bounded LLM pairwise judge with both-orderings aggregation rule as a new pipeline stage between S5 and S7) but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc capture of the decision.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## Dependencies sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (e.g., `docs/adr/0NNN-overlap-judge-aggregation.md`) covering: (1) why both orderings are required rather than one, (2) why abstain/low-confidence fails safe to non-surfaced rather than surfaced-with-warning, (3) the relation enum design and surfacing policy. Reference it from the plan's Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-call timeout (e.g., `overlap_judge_timeout_s` config field) so slow/hung LLM calls are bounded and trigger the abstain path rather than blocking indefinitely.
findings.finding: The plan specifies LLM errors trigger abstain but does not specify any timeout value for individual LLM calls, leaving the judge vulnerable to hanging indefinitely on a slow (non-erroring) LLM endpoint rather than failing fast to abstain.
findings.location: ## What — Error handling paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_judge_timeout_s` (default e.g. 30s) to `VerifyConfig` alongside the existing `overlap_*` fields, and specify that a call exceeding this deadline is treated identically to an LLM error (→ abstain, logged).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `contracts.py` registration mechanism (e.g., module-level idempotent dict assignment, decorator, or explicit `register_schema()` call) and confirm it is safe under repeated import/re-registration.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `overlap_verdict` is to be 'registered in `contracts.py`' but does not define the registration mechanism (module-level dict mutation, decorator, explicit init call, etc.) or when in the application lifecycle that registration occurs. This leaves the CREATE phase of this shared-state schema registry underspecified, making it unclear whether import-order dependencies or re-registration races are possible.
findings.location: Scope section: '`overlap_verdict` schema + `contracts.py` registration'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'Schema registration is a module-level idempotent assignment (`contracts.SCHEMAS["overlap_verdict"] = ...`); a duplicate registration of the same schema is a no-op (idempotent).' This satisfies both the CREATE definition and the concurrency/retry safety requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite gate: confirm `OverlapCandidate` (from S5) is importable from its canonical module path before this plan's implementation begins, or declare S5 as a hard prerequisite that must land first.
findings.finding: `OverlapCandidate` does not exist anywhere in the codebase (`src/` grep returns zero hits), yet the plan's Dependencies section asserts 'S5 (`OverlapCandidate` interface + the query/corpus digests)' as a ready dependency this plan consumes. S5 is explicitly OUT OF SCOPE for this plan. An executor importing `OverlapCandidate` from the expected location will fail with `ImportError` until S5 ships.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'S5 (`OverlapCandidate` interface + the query/corpus digests). Consumed by S7.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Dependencies: 'PREREQUISITE: S5 must be merged and `OverlapCandidate` importable from `rebar.llm.overlap.retrieve` (or its canonical S5 path) before this plan's implementation begins.' In the Testing section, note that `FakeRunner` tests should stub `OverlapCandidate` as a simple dataclass (e.g. `@dataclass class OverlapCandidate: id: str; digest: dict`) if S5 has not landed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a local stub or `Protocol` for `OverlapCandidate` within this plan's deliverables (e.g. in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py`) so `judge.py` is importable before S5 lands, or explicitly state the import path once S5 is merged.
findings.finding: `OverlapCandidate` — the primary symbol the plan imports and iterates over in `judge.py` — does not exist anywhere in the codebase. S5 is explicitly marked out-of-scope for this plan, yet `OverlapCandidate` is named as a concrete, importable dependency. Every call-site in `judge.py` that types or constructs an `OverlapCandidate` references a hallucinated symbol.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'S5 (`OverlapCandidate` interface + the query/corpus digests)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `OverlapCandidate` `TypedDict` or `dataclass` stub to `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` (fields: `ticket_id: str`, `query_digest: dict`, `candidate_digest: dict`) so the judge is self-contained and tests can run independently of S5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale (or pointer to the referenced 'research') for why the strict both-orderings-must-agree aggregation rule is preferred over score-averaging or single-ordering alternatives, either inline in the plan's ## Why section or as a linked ADR/experiment doc.
findings.finding: The dual-ordering aggregation rule (surface ONLY if BOTH orderings agree on the SAME surfaceable relation AND each cites a non-null shared_artifact AND both confidence >= CONF_THRESHOLD) is described as what 'the research converged on,' but no pointer to that research exists anywhere in docs/, experiments/, or an ADR, and no justification is given versus simpler established alternatives such as score-averaging across orderings, a single ordering with a tighter threshold, or a majority-vote scheme. This is a novel architectural choice with meaningful cost implications (2K calls/query vs K) that requires prior-art grounding per the T1 criterion.
findings.location: ## What — AGGREGATION RULE paragraph; ## Why
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the ## Why or ## What section citing the prior art (e.g., 'Following the MT-Bench / Chatbot Arena finding that position bias is eliminated when both A→B and B→A agree [link]') and note why score-averaging was rejected (e.g., 'averaging allows a high-confidence wrong-order call to pull through a low-confidence correct-order call, whereas unanimous agreement is strictly more conservative').

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
